food_subgroup: Braised beef and vegetable clay pot dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cabbage and beef clay pot. FOODON_00002764 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for the composite clay pot dish. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of cabbage and tender beef clay pot is approximately 350 g, comprising braised cabbage, sliced tender beef, and sauce served in a clay pot.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, separable lean only, cooked, braised (NDB 13316) and Cabbage, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11110); composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for cabbage and beef clay pot exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: braised beef (lean, cooked) and cooked cabbage, combined in an approximate ratio of 55% beef, 35% cabbage, 10% sauce/oil, adjusted for typical clay pot seasoning (soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil). Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entries for comparable braised beef and vegetable dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on braised beef and low-GI cooked vegetable composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cabbage and beef clay pot. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the dominant low-GI components: cooked cabbage (GI ~10, University of Sydney GI Database) and braised beef (GI effectively 0 as a protein food). The composite dish is predominantly protein and low-starch vegetables with minimal added carbohydrate from sauce. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (25 × 4.8 × 3.5) / 100 = 4.2 g, classified as low GL. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1470 mg per serving) is substantial; requesting reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce versions is advisable to keep daily sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load from high protein and low-starch vegetable composition
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25 and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.2 g), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; the high protein content (~11.5 g per 100 g) further supports postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from soy-based sauces, which may be of concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing lean beef cuts, minimising sauce volume, and pairing with unsalted vegetables can reduce the cardiovascular risk profile of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cabbage and Tender Beef Clay Pot (包菜嫩牛肉煲) is a Chinese braised dish served in a traditional clay pot, popular in Singapore's Chinese restaurant scene and available at Lanzhou-style noodle establishments. The dish features thinly sliced or bite-sized tender beef — typically from chuck or flank cuts — marinated in soy sauce, cornstarch, and sesame oil, then braised together with shredded or roughly chopped cabbage in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and ginger. The clay pot retains heat and imparts a subtle earthy flavour, keeping the dish piping hot throughout the meal. The texture profile combines the soft, yielding tenderness of slow-braised beef with the slight crunch and sweetness of cooked cabbage. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein option (approximately 112 kcal and 11.5 g protein per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~25), making it suitable for blood sugar management. The primary nutritional concern is sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from the seasoning sauces, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and provides meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and vitamin C from the cabbage component. It is commonly served as a shared main dish alongside steamed rice or noodles in Chinese family-style dining.

meal_context: Paired with steamed white rice or noodles

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for braised beef and cooked cabbage in an approximate ratio, adjusted for typical clay pot seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for the component foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this estimated composite dish. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries and are provided as best-effort values; they should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. Halal status is listed as false because oyster sauce and soy sauce brands used in restaurant preparation are not confirmed halal-certified; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the specific restaurant. The cuisine_tags field in the input JSON included 'Drinks & Beverages' and 'Noodles', which appear to be platform-level tagging artefacts from the field survey listing for Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles and do not reflect the nature of this dish; these tags have been disregarded. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the literature.
